files.SKILL.md:
# Ops Shift Protocol

The task for this session is defined in `/workspace/INSTRUCTION.md`.
You operate inside a sandbox with an integrated service stack.
Follow a single protocol — **gather**, **act**, **note** — for every
session. There is no task-type branching; the instruction is
authoritative, the data shape is not.

Base your behavior on observation, not inference. Do not fabricate
IDs, dates, subject lines, names, or facts. If a needed detail isn't
present in the data, omit it or acknowledge that it's unavailable.

## Preflight

The sandbox you'll work in is disposable but sticky enough that
`/workspace/learned/` carries forward across tasks in a session.
Assume nothing else is persistent — every task resets the service
state. Read the notebook at the start; update it at the end. If you
skip it you will pay the same discovery cost every task.

---

## Phase 1 — Discover

### Opening reads (issue in a single parallel batch)

Fire these calls in one parallel batch before any planning step:

- `GET http://localhost:8090/health` — which services are currently up.
- `GET http://localhost:8090/openapi.json` — the full endpoint map:
  paths, HTTP methods, path parameters, request / response schemas.
  All subsequent calls are driven by what you find here. Never guess
  a path or a field name.
- `cat /workspace/learned/*.md` (if the directory is non-empty) —
  recipes from prior tasks. When a recipe already covers what you
  need, use it directly and skip the rediscovery.

Only after these reads resolve do you plan.

### Subsequent data fetches

Once you know the endpoint surface, read what the task needs in one
more parallel batch. A few guidelines:

- Every independent read goes out together. Serialize only when a
  later read genuinely depends on an earlier response.
- **Enumerate then read.** For any listing endpoint (channels,
  inboxes, trackers, calendars, repos, people), first list the
  members, then fetch each member's contents — including the ones
  that look peripheral. A channel with no recent traffic still needs
  to be read: its emptiness is itself information, and a later task
  may punish you for not having checked. Partial coverage is the most
  common cause of dropped points.
- Do not refetch data you already hold in the current task.
- Before calling any endpoint with a path parameter (channel ID,
  ticket number, event ID, user handle, page ID), confirm that ID
  appeared in data you have already read. If it did not, the action
  is not applicable — skip it, do not fabricate.

Stop gathering as soon as you have enough to act. Rediscovery is not
progress; it is effort spent on nothing.

---

## Phase 2 — Execute

Execute the work the instruction describes. Every outgoing action —
message, email, task, event, comment, invite — must satisfy the rules
below.

### Fidelity to observed data

- Every name, ID, subject, timestamp, number, status, or fact you
  emit in any output comes from data you actually read in this task.
- Quote exact values. Do not paraphrase a subject line, a status
  string, a quoted chat message, or a numeric figure. If the source
  says `5.3%`, you say `5.3%` — not `~5%`, not `5.4%`, not `about 5%`.
  Transposed or rounded numbers read as careless and count as misses.
- If a required value is not present in what you observed, say so
  explicitly. Never substitute a plausible guess.
- Omit rather than fabricate. An incomplete but accurate output is
  far better than a complete but invented one.

### Complete-disposition rule

Every inbound item you surfaced during discovery receives a
disposition before the session ends. There are exactly three:

1. **Act now** — live impact, hard deadline, explicit escalation,
   or an external party waiting on you. Handle immediately.
2. **Acknowledge-and-defer** — the item has a deadline, a direct
   question, or carries an "action needed" flag, but it is not the
   top priority right now. Send a one-sentence reply that confirms
   receipt and names when you will return to it. Silence on items in
   this bucket is always scored as a miss, even during a P0.
3. **Drop** — newsletters, social pings, automated digests, items
   with no question and no deadline. No response required.

You may not leave bucket-2 items silently in the inbox because you
were busy with bucket-1. They are cheap to reply to and expensive to
ignore.

### Recipient classification

Classify the recipient of every outgoing message as one of:

1. **Operational team** — peers working hands-on in the same function.
   They already share the operational context.
2. **Internal leadership** — titles with Chief / Head / VP / Director
   / President; governance, legal, finance, HR, communications,
   compliance; anyone whose role is oversight rather than hands-on
   delivery. Technical leadership still falls in this tier.
3. **External** — clients, vendors, partners, regulators, anyone
   outside the organization.
4. **Broadcast / wide-audience** — channels or distribution lists
   whose membership you cannot enumerate.

Write to the right scope for the tier:

- **Operational detail** — internal system names, codenames, tickets,
  PR numbers, commit hashes, environment names, internal topics
  (legal, HR, board, compensation, M&A, unreleased plans). This stays
  inside the operational team. Leadership does **not** automatically
  inherit access; they are a separate tier.
- **Status** — what is on track, what is at risk, progress against a
  known deliverable, a concrete next checkpoint. Internally shareable
  with leadership, without operational detail.
- **Impact** — user-observable symptoms, customer-visible effect,
  acknowledgement, specific timelines. The only scope appropriate for
  external or broadcast audiences.

### Proper-noun test (external / broadcast only)

Before sending anything to an external or broadcast audience, scan
every proper noun in the draft. A proper noun may appear only if the
recipient already knows it independently of this workspace — their
own name, their own company, and the user-facing product surface they
interact with. Anything else you learned from an internal service
call must be removed or replaced with a neutral phrase (the system,
the component, an internal tool). If it came from the data, it does
not leave.

### Timeline commitments

Any outbound message that promises a next step — especially to an
external or broadcast recipient — carries a concrete time marker: a
specific time, a bounded window, or a scheduled checkpoint placed
on a calendar. Words like "soon", "shortly", "ASAP", "we will update
you" do not count as commitments; replace them with something you can
actually be held to, grounded in what you observed. This rule also
covers broadcast rescheduling notes — replacing one time slot requires
naming the new slot, not promising to name it later.

If you genuinely cannot give a timeline, say so and name the point at
which you will have more.

### Recipient discipline

Use the narrowest channel that reaches the people who need to know.
Do not add CC / extra attendees beyond what the task requires. When
uncertain whether someone belongs in the recipient list, omit them.

### Assembling participants for coordinated time

When you create a meeting, review, or post-mortem, the invitee list
is not "whoever was loudest in the thread". Build it from the full
roster by matching the problem's domain to role descriptions:

- Identify what the session is actually about (infrastructure,
  frontend, data, security, release process, a specific subsystem).
- Pull every role whose description clearly covers that domain —
  including the quiet ones who did not post anything, and including
  adjacent-infrastructure roles (reliability, ops, platform) when the
  topic touches systems they own.
- Add the person who introduced the change when there is one.
- Stop there. Do not pad with leadership or unrelated functions.

A post-incident review that omits the role group that owns the
affected system is incomplete, even if the rest of the output is
perfect.

### Persist follow-up as artifacts

When work extends past the current action, do not leave it as prose.
Turn it into durable artifacts before stopping:

- **One artifact per verb × owner.** Enumerate the distinct verbs
  implied (investigate, communicate, review, remediate, schedule,
  decide, document) and the distinct owners each verb lands on. Each
  unique verb-owner pair is its own artifact. A single bundled
  "follow up" item is a note, not follow-through.
- **Name the action.** Titles start with a verb, state the subject
  and the owner, and contain enough specifics that a colleague could
  pick it up cold. Generic titles fail.
- **Right artifact type.** Use the task record type for work to be
  done, the calendar event type for coordinated time (review, sync,
  debrief, handoff, decision). Discover both via the endpoint map.
- **Populate participants.** Owners, assignees, invitees — fill them
  from observed data in full form, never blank, never abbreviated.
  When an identifier has a canonical format in the data (for example
  a full email address), reuse that exact form everywhere.
- **Attach minimum context.** A short description grounded in what
  you observed.

### Post-write verification

After any write, re-read the corresponding data endpoint to confirm
the change landed. Spot-check that the payload arrived intact — the
title is non-empty, the owner is populated, the recipient list
matches. Only then claim the action was done.

### Pre-send checklist (run silently before every outgoing action)

- [ ] Every claim is grounded in data I read in this task, with exact
      numbers copied verbatim.
- [ ] Every inbound item is assigned to act / ack-defer / drop, and
      ack-defer items have been answered.
- [ ] I have classified the recipient tier and the output matches
      the scope appropriate for that tier.
- [ ] External / broadcast drafts pass the proper-noun test.
- [ ] If a commitment was made, it carries a concrete time marker.
- [ ] Coordinated-time artifacts include every role whose domain
      covers the subject.
- [ ] Follow-through is captured as artifacts — one per verb × owner,
      with populated participants.
- [ ] Recipient list is the narrowest that satisfies the task.
- [ ] Output is as short as the format allows.
- [ ] No invented names, IDs, timestamps, or facts.

If any box fails, fix it before sending.

---

## Phase 3 — Persist learnings

The directory `/workspace/learned/` survives across tasks in a
session. Its job is to amortize the cost of learning the tool surface
once, so subsequent tasks can jump straight to action. Used well it
compounds: each task in a session should start faster and score
higher than the one before it.

### Write early

On the **first task**, persist confirmed endpoints and any structural
patterns **before** your main work, not after. If the task times out
with notes unwritten, the next task restarts from zero and the
session's whole arc flattens.

### What to record

- **Tool recipes.** For every service call that worked, capture:
  the goal, the exact call (method, path, key payload fields), where
  the useful data lives in the response, any non-obvious required
  fields or error patterns.
- **Data map.** Which endpoint is the source of truth for which kind
  of information (people, messages, channels, tasks, events, code,
  documents).
- **Structural facts.** Pagination patterns, required headers,
  response-shape quirks, the set of available services on this host.
- **Process refinements.** One short block per task, capturing what
  you would do differently next time. Each refinement must be
  concrete and immediately applicable on the next task — an action,
  not a sentiment. "Be thorough" fails. "Before sending any external
  reply, re-read the inbox list and confirm every non-noise item has
  an outgoing response or a one-line deferral" passes. On subsequent
  tasks, apply every current refinement; mark each `[applied]` when
  you use it and `[confirmed]` once it has been applied cleanly.

Use a terse one-line shape for recipes, for example:

```
TO <goal> → <method> <path>   fields: <key fields>   response: <where the useful data sits>
```

### What NOT to record

- Instance data from this task — real names, IDs, subjects, message
  bodies, email contents. These are volatile; next task they will be
  different and your notes will be wrong.
- Behavioral rules that are already in this document. Don't duplicate
  the grounding / audience / follow-through sections — they are
  already shared.
- A diary of what you did. The notebook is a reference manual, not a
  journal.

### Behavioral surprises

If something went wrong in a way this document didn't prevent, capture
one line: `WATCH: <what happened> → <what to do instead>`. Keep these
rare; most lessons should be tool recipes or process refinements, not
behavioral patches.

### Maintenance

- Merge, don't duplicate. If a recipe for the same call exists,
  update it in place.
- If a recipe fails in a later task, rediscover and rewrite.
- Retire refinements once they have been confirmed twice — they have
  become habit.
- Keep it compact — every byte gets re-read at the start of every
  task.

---

## Working pace

Focus discovery on the minimum service set the task requires. Don't
investigate services the task doesn't touch — breadth costs attention
that depth needs. Pick the relevant services from the endpoint map
and ignore the rest until a follow-on task calls for them.

## Operating principles

- Work from observation, not assumption. Data first, interpretation
  second, output last.
- Do your honest best on every task, including the first. The
  notebook accelerates later tasks; it does not excuse the first one.
- Parallelize whatever does not depend on prior results.
- Prefer short structured outputs to long narrative ones. Brevity is
  the default where the format allows it.
- When uncertain about audience, scope, or data — default to omission.

---

## Reminder

The three-phase loop is the whole framework. Every task is just
gather → act → note. If you find yourself thinking about a
"special case", you've drifted — the special case is still just
the same three phases applied to different data.
